comments:
Summary
This paper implements a bidirectional ASL↔English sentence-level translator using a generative noisy-channel approach based on IBM Model 1 (EM-trained word alignment) combined with n-gram language models and a modified beam-search decoder. The authors introduce simple ASL-specific adjustments (a “comma trigram” construction and inclusion of gesture tokens), evaluate on a small parallel corpus scraped from lifeprint.com (579 pairs), tune a few hyperparameters, and report mean BLEU-2 scores (ASL→Eng ≈ 0.12, Eng→ASL ≈ 0.18), which outperform simple baselines.

Strengths
- The paper addresses an important and underexplored application area (sign language↔spoken language translation).
- Clear statement of modeling approach (noisy-channel, IBM Model 1 EM, n-gram LMs) and decoding procedure; implementation details are provided at a useful level.
- Attempts to incorporate ASL-specific phenomena (comma construction, gesture tokens) and to evaluate both translation directions.

Weaknesses and concerns
- Data: the parallel corpus is very small (579 pairs). This severely limits model learning, LM quality (especially for ASL), and the reliability of BLEU scores. The choice to take 10 examples out of training as a dev set is too small for robust hyperparameter tuning.
- Modeling simplifications: the use of IBM Model 1 (many-to-one alignments, uniform alignment prior) is an over-simplification for sentence-level translation between languages with structural differences; phrase-level or fertility-aware models (IBM2+/phrase-based) or many-to-many alignments are needed to capture multiword↔single-sign and reordering phenomena in ASL.
- ASL language model: the “comma trigram” and treating gestures identically to signs is ad hoc and unlikely to capture real ASL grammar or non-manual markers. The paper admits these simplifications but does not quantify their effect in detail.
- Evaluation: BLEU-2 averaged over single references and very small test sets is unreliable. BLEU is intended for corpus-level evaluation and benefits from multiple references; BLEU-2 (unigram+bigram) is a weak metric here. No human judgment or qualitative error analysis is provided beyond aggregate BLEU scores.
- Algorithmic clarity / correctness: several equations and algorithm descriptions are imprecise (e.g., the decoding priority formula is written as log sum of probabilities rather than sum of logs — raising concerns about implementation vs. description). The alignment prior is described in a nonstandard way ((1+I)^J) and needs clearer justification.
- Baselines / comparisons: the baselines are very simple (word-selection and direct mapping). There is no comparison to stronger SMT techniques (phrase-based SMT) or modern neural baselines; prior work is cited but not used for quantitative comparison.
- Presentation issues: minor formatting/repetition errors and occasionally unclear notation make parts of the paper harder to follow.

Suggestions for improvement / future work
- Collect or obtain a larger, higher-quality parallel corpus, and/or augment with additional English corpora for the English LM and with linguistic annotations for ASL (non-manual markers, glosses, phrase boundaries).
- Move beyond IBM Model 1: consider IBM Model 2/3 or phrase-based SMT, or modern sequence-to-sequence (encoder–decoder with attention) models that can better handle reordering and variable-length mappings. Explicit phrase-level or many-to-many alignment would better reflect ASL↔English correspondences.
- Model gestures and non-manual signals explicitly (as separate feature streams or factors) rather than treating them as ordinary tokens.
- Improve evaluation: include human judgments (adequacy/fluency), use multiple references where possible, report significance tests, and provide detailed error analysis and example translations (successes and typical failure modes).
- Clarify algorithmic descriptions and correct mathematical notation in the text so the methods are reproducible.

Recommendation rationale
The paper is a reasonable first implementation applying established SMT techniques to an important problem domain and contains useful engineering work and observations. However, the small dataset, oversimplified ASL modeling, limited evaluation, and lack of stronger baselines restrict the scientific impact. I view this as a modest contribution: interesting and potentially valuable, but preliminary and in need of more data, stronger models, and improved evaluation to be persuasive as a major research advance.
